turn_a: I often wonder, Socrates, that Gorgias is never heard promising to teach virtue: and when he hears others promising he only laughs at them; but he thinks that men should be taught to speak.
turn_b: Then do you not think that the Sophists are teachers?
prompt: I often wonder, the old teacher, that the teacher of speaking is never heard say they will teach being good: and when he hears others say they will he only laughs at them; but he thinks that men should be taught to speak.
response: Then do you not think that the people who teach for money are teachers?

turn_a: Why, Socrates, even now I am not able to follow you in the attempt to get at one common notion of virtue as of other things.
turn_b: No wonder; but I will try to get nearer if I can, for you know that all things have a common notion. Suppose now that some one asked you the question which I asked before: Meno, he would say, what is figure? And if you answered 'roundness,' he would reply to you, in my way of speaking, by asking whether you would say that roundness is 'figure' or 'a figure;' and you would answer 'a figure.'
prompt: Why old teacher, even now I cannot follow you as you try to find one same idea of good like other things?
response: That makes sense; but I will try to get closer if I can, because you know that all things have a same idea. Imagine now that some one asks you the question I asked before: the person we asked before would say, what is shape? And if you answer "round shape," the person we asked before would answer in my talk by asking if you would say that round shape is shape or a shape; and you would answer "a shape."

turn_a: I should answer that bees do not differ from one another, as bees.
turn_b: And if I went on to say: That is what I desire to know, Meno; tell me what is the quality in which they do not differ, but are all alike;--would you be able to answer?
prompt: I should answer that the little things that fly and make sweet food are not different from one another, as the little things that fly and make sweet food.
response: And if I went on to say: That is what I want to know, the young man; tell me what is the thing that they are not different in, but are all the same; would you be able to answer?

turn_a: And therefore not by any wisdom, and not because they were wise, did Themistocles and those others of whom Anytus spoke govern states. This was the reason why they were unable to make others like themselves--because their virtue was not grounded on knowledge.
turn_b: That is probably true, Socrates.
prompt: Not because they had smart thoughts, and not because they were smart, the great head of long ago and the other people the man who talked about the past said did not run places. That is why they could not help other people be like them because their being good was not based on what people know.
response: That is probably true, the old teacher.

turn_a: Yes, Socrates; and we were quite right in doing so.
turn_b: But then, my friend, do not suppose that we can explain to any one the nature of virtue as a whole through some unexplained portion of virtue, or anything at all in that fashion; we should only have to ask over again the old question, What is virtue? Am I not right?
prompt: Yes, the old teacher; and we were very right in doing that.
response: But the young man, do not think that we can tell any person what the right way is as all by using a little part of the right way that we do not know or any thing like that; we should only have to ask again the old question, what is the right way? am I not right?

turn_a: Why, how can there be virtue without these?
turn_b: And the non-acquisition of gold and silver in a dishonest manner for oneself or another, or in other words the want of them, may be equally virtue?
prompt: Why, how can there be good without these?
response: And not get shiny metal things by not honest for you or for another, or in other words not have them, could also be good?

turn_a: And the goods which you mean are such as health and wealth and the possession of gold and silver, and having office and honour in the state--those are what you would call goods?
turn_b: Yes, I should include all those.
prompt: And the good things you talk about are things like being well, having a lot of money, having bright things, having a job and respect in the city - are those the good things you call good?
response: Yes, I would add all of those.

turn_a: You are outrageous, Meno, in thus plaguing a poor old man to give you an answer, when you will not take the trouble of remembering what is Gorgias' definition of virtue.
turn_b: When you have told me what I ask, I will tell you, Socrates.
prompt: You are wrong, the young man, for making trouble for a weak old man to get an answer, when you will not take the work of remembering what the famous teacher of speaking said about being good.
response: When you have told me what I want to know, I will tell you, the old teacher.

turn_a: Courage and temperance and wisdom and magnanimity are virtues; and there are many others.
turn_b: Yes, Meno; and again we are in the same case: in searching after one virtue we have found many, though not in the same way as before; but we have been unable to find the common virtue which runs through them all.
prompt: being brave and self control and being smart and being kind are good ways; and there are many others.
response: Yes, the young man; and again we are in the same case: in looking for one good way we have found many, though not in the same way as before; but we have not been able to find the one good way that is in all of them.

turn_a: Not if we are right in our view. But I cannot believe, Socrates, that there are no good men: And if there are, how did they come into existence?
turn_b: I am afraid, Meno, that you and I are not good for much, and that Gorgias has been as poor an educator of you as Prodicus has been of me. Certainly we shall have to look to ourselves, and try to find some one who will help in some way or other to improve us. This I say, because I observe that in the previous discussion none of us remarked that right and good action is possible to man under other guidance than that of knowledge (episteme);--and indeed if this be denied, there is no seeing how there can be any good men at all.
prompt: Not if we think we are right. But I can not think, the old teacher, that there are no good people: And if there are, how did they come to be?
response: I do not think, the young man, that you and me are not much good, and that the teacher of talk has been as bad a teacher for you as the teacher of words has been for me. We will have to look at us, and try to find a person who will help in some way to make us better. I say this because I see that in the last talk none of us said that a right and good thing to do can be for a person unless it comes from what someone knows; and if that is said no, we can not see how any good people can be.

turn_a: Why single out individuals? Any Athenian gentleman, taken at random, if he will mind him, will do far more good to him than the Sophists.
turn_b: And did those gentlemen grow of themselves; and without having been taught by any one, were they nevertheless able to teach others that which they had never learned themselves?
prompt: Why pick people? Any person from the city, if you pick him, will do much more help to him than the people who teach for money.
response: And did those men grow on their own; and without being taught by any one, could they still teach other people things they had never learned?

turn_a: Yes, Socrates; I agree there; for justice is virtue.
turn_b: Would you say 'virtue,' Meno, or 'a virtue'?
prompt: Yes, the old teacher; I agree; because the right way is being good.
response: Would you say being good, the young man, or a being good?

turn_a: To be sure I should.
turn_b: And did not he train his son Lysimachus better than any other Athenian in all that could be done for him by the help of masters? But what has been the result? Is he a bit better than any other mortal? He is an acquaintance of yours, and you see what he is like. There is Pericles, again, magnificent in his wisdom; and he, as you are aware, had two sons, Paralus and Xanthippus.
prompt: I should be sure.
response: And did not he train his son the boy of the great head of long ago better than any other person from the city in all that could be done for him by the help of teachers? But what has been the result? Is he a little better than any other person? He is a friend of yours, and you see what he is like. There is the great head of long ago, again, great in his smart; and he, as you know, had two boys, the first boy and the second boy.

turn_a: Yes, that appears to be the question which comes next in order.
turn_b: Do we not say that virtue is a good?--This is a hypothesis which is not set aside.
prompt: Yes, that looks like the next question.
response: Do we not say that good is the right way? This is an idea that we do not put away.

turn_a: I am beginning to understand; but I do not as yet take hold of the question as I could wish.
turn_b: When you say, Meno, that there is one virtue of a man, another of a woman, another of a child, and so on, does this apply only to virtue, or would you say the same of health, and size, and strength? Or is the nature of health always the same, whether in man or woman?
prompt: I am beginning to understand, but I do not yet hold the question as I wish.
response: When you say, the young man, that there is one being good for a man, another for a woman, another for a child, and so on, does this work only for being good, or would you say the same for well, for how big, and for how strong? Or is the way things are of well always the same if in man or woman?

turn_a: And so of the virtues, however many and different they may be, they have all a common nature which makes them virtues; and on this he who would answer the question, 'What is virtue?' would do well to have his eye fixed: Do you understand?
turn_b: I am beginning to understand; but I do not as yet take hold of the question as I could wish.
prompt: And so about the being good things, however many and different they are, they all have one common way that makes them being good things; and on this anyone who would answer the question, "What is being good?" would do well to keep his eye fixed. Do you understand?
response: I am beginning to understand, but I do not yet get the question as I would wish.

turn_a: I mean as I might say about anything; that a round, for example, is 'a figure' and not simply 'figure,' and I should adopt this mode of speaking, because there are other figures.
turn_b: Quite right; and that is just what I am saying about virtue--that there are other virtues as well as justice.
prompt: I say it like this for any thing; for example, a round shape is a shape and not just shape, and I should use this way of talking because there are other shapes.
response: That is right; and that is what I am saying about being good -- that there are other being good things as also the right way.

turn_a: Then, according to your definition, virtue would appear to be the power of attaining good?
turn_b: I entirely approve, Socrates, of the manner in which you now view this matter.
prompt: So, if we use your words, being good seems to be the way to get good?
response: I fully like the old teacher for the way you now see this thing.

turn_a: Then, according to Meno, who is the hereditary friend of the great king, virtue is the power of getting silver and gold; and would you add that they must be gained piously, justly, or do you deem this to be of no consequence? And is any mode of acquisition, even if unjust and dishonest, equally to be deemed virtue?
turn_b: Not virtue, Socrates, but vice.
prompt: Then as the young man who is close friend of big king the right way is to get gold and silver and would you say they must be got in a good way or do you think that does not count And is any way of getting even if not right and not true also the right way
response: Not the right way the old teacher but bad

turn_a: Out of their minds! No, Socrates; the young men who gave their money to them were out of their minds, and their relations and guardians who entrusted their youth to the care of these men were still more out of their minds, and most of all, the cities who allowed them to come in, and did not drive them out, citizen and stranger alike.
turn_b: Has any of the Sophists wronged you, Anytus? What makes you so angry with them?
prompt: No! They are not right. The young men who gave their money to them were not right, and the family and those who watch who gave their young ones to the care of these men were even more not right, and most of all, the place that let them in and did not send them out, the people who live there and the people who do not live there.
response: Has any of the people who teach for money hurt you, the angry man? What makes you so mad with them?

turn_a: Now, can there be a doubt that Thucydides, whose children were taught things for which he had to spend money, would have taught them to be good men, which would have cost him nothing, if virtue could have been taught? Will you reply that he was a mean man, and had not many friends among the Athenians and allies? Nay, but he was of a great family, and a man of influence at Athens and in all Hellas, and, if virtue could have been taught, he would have found out some Athenian or foreigner who would have made good men of his sons, if he could not himself spare the time from cares of state. Once more, I suspect, friend Anytus, that virtue is not a thing which can be taught?
turn_b: Socrates, I think that you are too ready to speak evil of men: and, if you will take my advice, I would recommend you to be careful. Perhaps there is no city in which it is not easier to do men harm than to do them good, and this is certainly the case at Athens, as I believe that you know.
prompt: Now can there be a doubt that the old war writer of long ago whose kids were taught stuff for which he had to spend money would have taught them to be good people which would cost him nothing if the right way could have been taught? Will you reply that he was a bad person and had not many friends among the people of the city and allies? No but he was of a big family and a man of power at the city and in all the whole land and if the right way could have been taught he would have found out some city person or foreign person who would have made his kids good people of his sons if he could not himself spare the time from work of government. Once more I suspect friend that the right way is not a thing which can be taught
response: the angry man says the old teacher I think you are too ready to speak bad of people and if you will take my advice I would suggest you be safe. Perhaps there is no city in which it is not easy to hurt people than to do them good and this is sure the case at the city as I believe you know

turn_a: And I think, Socrates, that they are right; although very likely our friend Anytus may take offence at the word.
turn_b: I do not care; as for Anytus, there will be another opportunity of talking with him. To sum up our enquiry--the result seems to be, if we are at all right in our view, that virtue is neither natural nor acquired, but an instinct given by God to the virtuous. Nor is the instinct accompanied by reason, unless there may be supposed to be among statesmen some one who is capable of educating statesmen. And if there be such an one, he may be said to be among the living what Homer says that Tiresias was among the dead, 'he alone has understanding; but the rest are flitting shades'; and he and his virtue in like manner will be a reality among shadows.
prompt: And I think, the old teacher, that they are right; but maybe our friend the man we know will feel bad at the word.
response: I do not care; and about the man we know we will have another chance to talk with him. In short, our look seems to say that if we are right, being good is not something we are born with or learn, but a deep feel given by God to people who are good. This deep feel does not come with thinking, if there is one head who can teach other heads. If such a one is, he is like the story about the blind man who can see among the dead: he alone knows; the rest are just dead. He and his being good will be a real thing among dead.

turn_a: Certainly; no man better.
turn_b: And must not he then have been a good teacher, if any man ever was a good teacher, of his own virtue?
prompt: yes; no person better.
response: And must not the angry man then have been a good person who shows how to learn, if any person ever was a good person who shows how to learn, because of his own being good?

turn_a: How can it be otherwise?
turn_b: But are not the miserable ill-fated?
prompt: How can it be different?
response: But are they not sad and have bad luck?

turn_a: And are you aware that not you only and other politicians have doubts whether virtue can be taught or not, but that Theognis the poet says the very same thing?
turn_b: Where does he say so?
prompt: And do you know that not only you but also other people who run the city are not sure if the right way can be taught or not, and that the old song person of long ago tell the same thing?
response: Where does the old song person of long ago say that?

turn_a: What you are saying, Socrates, seems to be very like the truth.
turn_b: I too speak rather in ignorance; I only conjecture. And yet that knowledge differs from true opinion is no matter of conjecture with me. There are not many things which I profess to know, but this is most certainly one of them.
prompt: What you are saying, the old teacher, seems to be very like the truth.
response: I too speak rather in ignorance; I only guess. And yet that what someone knows is not the same as right thought; it is not a guess for me. There are not many things that I say I know, but this is most sure one of those.

turn_a: The next question is, whether virtue is knowledge or of another species?
turn_b: Yes, that appears to be the question which comes next in order.
prompt: The next question is if being good is what someone knows or something else?
response: Yes, that looks like the next question we must ask.

turn_a: I entirely approve, Socrates, of the manner in which you now view this matter.
turn_b: Then let us see whether what you say is true from another point of view; for very likely you may be right:--You affirm virtue to be the power of attaining goods?
prompt: I really like the way the old teacher now thinks about this.
response: Then let us see if what you say is true from another side, for maybe you are right: you say being good is the power to get good things?

turn_a: I think that what you are saying, Socrates, is very true.
turn_b: But if this is true, then the good are not by nature good?
prompt: I think that what you are saying, the old teacher, is very true.
response: But if this is true, then the good are not by nature good?

turn_a: Here was a teacher of virtue whom you admit to be among the best men of the past. Let us take another,--Aristides, the son of Lysimachus: would you not acknowledge that he was a good man?
turn_b: To be sure I should.
prompt: Here was a teacher of being good who you say is one of the best people from long ago. Let us choose another, the well known man of long ago who is the child of the man who was a leader of long ago: would you not say that he was a good person?
response: I would.

turn_a: But then, my friend, do not suppose that we can explain to any one the nature of virtue as a whole through some unexplained portion of virtue, or anything at all in that fashion; we should only have to ask over again the old question, What is virtue? Am I not right?
turn_b: I believe that you are.
prompt: But then my friend do not think we can tell anyone what being good all together by using only a small not clear part of being good or anything like that we must ask again the old question what is being good Am I right?
response: I think you are right.

turn_a: Not only that, my dear boy, but you may say further that I have never known of any one else who did, in my judgment.
turn_b: Then you have never met Gorgias when he was at Athens?
prompt: Not only that, my dear boy, but you may say more that I have never known any other person who did, in what I think.
response: Then the old teacher has never met the well known teacher of words when he was in the town?

turn_a: I should like nothing better.
turn_b: Do not he and you and Empedocles say that there are certain effluences of existence?
prompt: I should like nothing better.
response: Do he and you and the old man of long ago say that there are some things that go out of life?

turn_a: I admit the cogency of your argument, and therefore, Socrates, I wonder that knowledge should be preferred to right opinion--or why they should ever differ.
turn_b: And shall I explain this wonder to you?
prompt: I admit the strong point of your talk, and so the old teacher, I wonder why what someone knows should be better than a good idea, or why they should ever be different?
response: And should I tell the young man about this wonder?

turn_a: Clearly, Socrates, it will be double.
turn_b: Do you observe, Meno, that I am not teaching the boy anything, but only asking him questions; and now he fancies that he knows how long a line is necessary in order to produce a figure of eight square feet; does he not?
prompt: It is clear, the old teacher, that it will be two parts.
response: Do you see, the young helper, that I am not showing the child any thing but just asking him questions and now he thinks he knows how long a line we need to make a shape that is eight foot of space right?

turn_a: Then do you not think that the Sophists are teachers?
turn_b: I cannot tell you, Socrates; like the rest of the world, I am in doubt, and sometimes I think that they are teachers and sometimes not.
prompt: Then do you not think that the people who teach for money are people who help learn, young man?
response: I can not tell you, the old teacher; like other people, I am not sure, and now and then I think that they are people who help learn and now and then not.

turn_a: Would you like me to answer you after the manner of Gorgias, which is familiar to you?
turn_b: I should like nothing better.
prompt: Would you like me to answer you after the way the teacher of talking that you know uses?
response: I would like nothing more.

turn_a: What do you mean by the word 'right'?
turn_b: I will explain. If a man knew the way to Larisa, or anywhere else, and went to the place and led others thither, would he not be a right and good guide?
prompt: What do you mean by the word right?
response: the old teacher will explain. If a man knew the way to the far town or any other place and went to that place and led others there would he not be a right and good person who leads?

turn_a: And now try and tell me the length of the line which forms the side of that double square: this is two feet--what will that be?
turn_b: Clearly, Socrates, it will be double.
prompt: Now tell me how long the line that is the side of the two squares together is if it is two feet, what will it be?
response: Yes, the old teacher, it will be two times as long.

turn_a: Yes, Socrates; and you are quite right in saying so.
turn_b: And am I not also right in saying that true opinion leading the way perfects action quite as well as knowledge?
prompt: Yes, the old teacher, and you are very right in saying that.
response: And am I not also right in saying that a right thought that guides the way makes what we do better like what someone knows?

turn_a: Then right opinion is not less useful than knowledge?
turn_b: The difference, Socrates, is only that he who has knowledge will always be right; but he who has right opinion will sometimes be right, and sometimes not.
prompt: Then a right thought is not less good for use than what someone knows?
response: The point, the old teacher, is only that the one who has what someone knows will always be right; but the one who has a right thought will sometimes be right, and sometimes not.

turn_a: Yes, they were all his own.
turn_b: And yet, as we were just now saying, he did not know?
prompt: Yes, they were all his own.
response: And still, as we were just now saying, he did not know?

turn_a: Quite right; and that is just what I am saying about virtue--that there are other virtues as well as justice.
turn_b: What are they? tell me the names of them, as I would tell you the names of the other figures if you asked me.
prompt: Quite right; that is just what I am saying about being good -- that there are other right ways as well as being fair.
response: What are they? tell me the names of them, as I would tell you the names of the other people if you asked me.

turn_a: I think not, Socrates.
turn_b: Then he was the better for the torpedo's touch?
prompt: I think not, the old teacher.
response: Then he was more good because of the fast thing under water's hit?

turn_a: But from what line?--tell me exactly; and if you would rather not reckon, try and show me the line.
turn_b: Indeed, Socrates, I do not know.
prompt: But from what line? Tell me exactly; and if you would not think, try to show me the line.
response: Indeed, the old teacher, I do not know.

turn_a: What of that! Why, did not I ask you to tell me the nature of virtue as a whole? And you are very far from telling me this; but declare every action to be virtue which is done with a part of virtue; as though you had told me and I must already know the whole of virtue, and this too when frittered away into little pieces. And, therefore, my dear Meno, I fear that I must begin again and repeat the same question: What is virtue? for otherwise, I can only say, that every action done with a part of virtue is virtue; what else is the meaning of saying that every action done with justice is virtue? Ought I not to ask the question over again; for can any one who does not know virtue know a part of virtue?
turn_b: No; I do not say that he can.
prompt: What about that! Did I not ask you to tell me what the right way is as a whole? You are far from that; you say every thing is the right way if it has a part of the right way, as if you had told me and I must now know the whole of the right way, also when it is cut into little parts. So, the young man, I think I must begin again and ask the same thing: What is the right way? If not, I can only say that every thing that has a part of the right way is the right way; what does it mean to say that every thing that has right is the right way? Should I not ask the same thing again; for can any person who does not know the right way know a part of the right way?
response: No; I do not say that the old teacher can.

turn_a: And will not virtue, as virtue, be the same, whether in a child or in a grown-up person, in a woman or in a man?
turn_b: I cannot help feeling, Socrates, that this case is different from the others.
prompt: Will good be the same whether in a child or in an adult, in a woman or in a man?
response: I can not help feel, the old teacher, that this thing is not the same as the other things.

turn_a: But if the good are not by nature good, are they made good by instruction?
turn_b: There appears to be no other alternative, Socrates. On the supposition that virtue is knowledge, there can be no doubt that virtue is taught.
prompt: But if the good are not by nature good, are they made good by teaching?
response: There looks to be no other way, the old teacher. If we think that being good is what someone knows, then we can not be sure that being good is taught.

turn_a: Why do you not tell him yourself?
turn_b: I have told him whom I supposed to be the teachers of these things; but I learn from you that I am utterly at fault, and I dare say that you are right. And now I wish that you, on your part, would tell me to whom among the Athenians he should go. Whom would you name?
prompt: Why do you not tell him yourself?
response: I have told him who I think should be the people who show how to do these things; but I learn from you that I am very wrong, and I think you are right. And now I wish that you would tell me which of the people of the city he should go to. Who would you name?

turn_a: Well, I will try and explain to you what figure is. What do you say to this answer?--Figure is the only thing which always follows colour. Will you be satisfied with it, as I am sure that I should be, if you would let me have a similar definition of virtue?
turn_b: But, Socrates, it is such a simple answer.
prompt: Well I will try to tell you what thing is. What do you think about this answer? Shape is the only thing that always comes after color. Will you be happy with it as I know I would be if you let me have the same what it means of being good?
response: But the old teacher, it is a very easy reply.
